name: Sinon.js
what_it_does: JavaScript test utilities library providing spies (call tracking), stubs (behavior replacement), mocks (expectation + stub), and fake timers (clock control). Sinon works standalone or with any test framework. Used to isolate units under test from dependencies — stub HTTP calls, fake timers, and track function invocations in agent test suites.
use_cases: Stub external API calls in agent unit tests — replace fetch/axios with sinon stubs that return controlled responses without network calls
Track function invocations with spies to verify agent workflows call downstream functions the correct number of times with correct arguments
Use fake timers (sinon.useFakeTimers) to test time-dependent agent logic like timeouts, retries with backoff, and scheduled tasks
Mock entire modules with sinon sandbox for isolated agent component testing with automatic cleanup via sandbox.restore()
Test error handling paths by stubbing functions to throw specific errors and verifying agent recovery logic responds correctly
not_for: New Jest projects — Jest has built-in jest.fn(), jest.spyOn(), and jest.useFakeTimers() that cover all Sinon use cases; no extra package needed
ES module mocking — Sinon's module mocking doesn't work well with native ESM; use testdouble.js or Jest's experimental ESM mocking
End-to-end testing — Sinon is for unit/integration test isolation, not E2E browser automation
best_when: You're using Mocha and need spies, stubs, and fake timers as a separate package from the assertion library.
avoid_when: You're using Jest — its built-in mocking is equivalent and requires no additional package.

pricing.notes: Sinon.js is open source and free.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — local test utility.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Stub assertion failures include call count, actual calls, and expected calls. Expectation mismatches show the full expected spec. Fake timer errors indicate what timer operations were pending.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Sandbox.restore() resets all stubs and spies — idempotent teardown. Always use sandboxes in afterEach to prevent test pollution.
known_agent_gotchas: Always use sinon.createSandbox() and call sandbox.restore() in afterEach — global sinon stubs that aren't restored leak into subsequent tests causing false positives
Sinon fake timers replace Date, setTimeout, setInterval globally — code using setImmediate or process.nextTick behaves differently with fake timers enabled
stub.returns() and stub.callsFake() apply to ALL calls — use stub.onCall(n) for call-count-specific behavior in sequence testing
Sinon can't stub ES module exports in Node.js native ESM — use dependency injection or CommonJS require() for stubbable module boundaries
Mock expectations must be verified explicitly with mock.verify() — unlike stubs, mock expectations don't auto-fail; forgetting verify() silently skips assertion
sinon.spy() on class methods requires passing the instance and method name — sinon.spy(instance, 'methodName') not sinon.spy(instance.method)

security_score_components.security_notes: Local test utility. Stubs that intercept HTTP calls in tests should not be accidentally left in production bundles — use test-only imports.
